input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A teleoperated 1/10‑scale autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology. The physical twin architecture consists of a lower and upper chassis coupled with autonomy elements, including an Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) system. The vehicle operates in a controlled environment for ACC development, making it suitable for digital twinning due to its small scale, dense sensor suite, and the ability to off‑load critical computational tasks to external resources.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Three primary services are provided by the Digital Twin system (DTS). 1) Data Monitoring – a user interface (UI) that visualises real‑time vehicle state data, projected driving path, and detected object tracks, and allows users to save and generate detailed reports. 2) Computation Offloading – a mechanism that transfers the ACC decision logic (lead‑vehicle detection, speed computation, and command generation) from the on‑board ECU to cloud or edge compute nodes, thereby relieving the constrained vehicle hardware. 3) Remote Management – real‑time remote control of ACC, including enable/disable, desired‑speed configuration, and emergency braking commands, all within strict low end‑to‑end latency constraints to preserve safety.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin operates with a 100 ms clock‑synchronization accuracy imposed by hardware limitations; devices use this shared time base to coordinate computation off‑loading. Network latency between the physical and digital twins is constrained to a maximum of 100 ms to satisfy the required real‑time performance. No finer temporal granularity or higher‑frequency synchronization is specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system adheres to an ISO 23247‑based reference architecture, structuring its software around three primary entities: User, Digital Twin, and Physical Twin. Each functional element is encapsulated as a ROS 2 package containing one or more ROS 2 nodes; this mapping aligns with the entity‑based hierarchy of ISO 23247. The User Entity hosts the User Interface FE, which is split into distinct 3D Visualization and Control Panel components. The Digital Twin Entity aggregates components responsible for data management and control mechanisms, such as data storage, while the Physical Twin Entity handles resource‑specific functionalities, including sensor/actuator interfaces and autonomous processing tasks like point‑cloud clustering and tracking. Deployment occurs across multiple hardware nodes, so ownership of each functional entity’s implementation may differ from the standard ISO 23247 definition, leading to deviations that affect engineering practices but preserve the overall architectural integrity. No additional data storage entity is listed; the component lacks a natural Functional Entity mapping but remains part of the Digital Twin Entity.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin constellation comprises a geometric point‑cloud clustering & tracking model implemented with a DBSCAN clustering algorithm followed by Kalman‑filter tracking; a behavioral Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) model that governs vehicle dynamics; and an analytical data‑storage model for historical event logs. Data sources for these models include a 3‑D LiDAR (e.g., Hokuyo UST–10LX) and an Intel D435i depth camera. Raw sensor streams are ingested via a ROS 2 middleware stack with Python and C++ nodes. The Data Collection and Device Control Entity gathers the raw data, passes it through a Data‑Preprocessing Functional Entity that performs segmentation and tracking, and delivers it to the Digital Twin Entity, which maintains the state of the observables.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation is performed by a Vedder Electronic Speed Controller driven by high‑frequency PWM signals generated by a low‑level control FE. Remote actuation commands are issued through a dedicated control panel that interfaces with the Data Collection and Device Control Entity. The system supports on‑the‑fly re‑calibration of the ESC and allows the User Entity to change throttle limits via the interface.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing subsystem consists of a Hokuyo UST‑10LX LiDAR scanner for 3‑D point‑cloud acquisition and an Intel D435i depth camera for RGB‑D streams. Both sensors provide range data that are aggregated by the Data Collection Entity. A Kalman filter within the Data‑Preprocessing FE fuses the raw measurements and produces tracking data for the Digital Twin.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Continuous data streams from physical sensors—LiDAR point cloud data, vehicle state metrics, motor and steering actuation signals—are forwarded to the Digital Twin system via a ROS 2 internal network for component‑to‑component communication and over a Wi‑Fi interface utilizing UDP for inter‑entity links. Prior to integration, the Data Translation component serialises all incoming streams, standardises message types and performs necessary unit conversions to ensure consistency. The raw LiDAR point cloud is pre‑processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software component, which extracts object tracks and projected driving paths. These pre‑processed streams are then fed into the virtual representation layers, enabling real‑time visualization in the 3D Visualization User Interface. Data arrival events trigger downstream processing, and type/ unit alignment by Data Translation provides a basic level of data quality assurance.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands from the DT (e.g., steering or motor actuation requests produced by the Control Panel FE) are dispatched through the Controlling FE. Commands are serialized into messages matching the vehicle’s actuator interface (likely a ROS2 topic over UDP via Wi‑Fi). The document does not detail explicit command formats, authentication, or validation mechanisms. Feedback is provided by periodically updated vehicle state signals (e.g., wheel speed, steering angle) that are re‑integrated into the DT. Conflict resolution and execution confirmation procedures are not described in the provided excerpts.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DT’s internal software network is built on ROS 2, providing message‑passing between SW components. For communication between the Physical Twin Entity and the Digital Twin Entity, a Wi‑Fi connection that carries UDP packets is employed. The document does not specify further details such as bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, or security protocols for this network.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system follows the ISO 23247‑2021 digital twin framework for manufacturing, implementing the prescribed reference architecture with entities for the Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), Data Collection and Device Control, Digital Twin, and User interfaces.  Each entity is composed of Functional Entities (FEs) that execute specific tasks such as OME actuation, data transformation into human‑interpretable forms, and user interaction.  Communication between FEs and the OME may be realized using OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) or similar interoperable industrial protocols, as exemplified by the reference implementation on GitHub (https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin) and its public demonstration video.  The approach aligns with ISO 23247’s interoperability and information‑exchange guidelines but does not prescribe specific tooling beyond the mentioned OPC UA usage.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Digital Twin aligns its safety architecture with ISO 15622, which specifies the performance requirements and test procedures for Adaptive Cruise Control systems.  Per ISO 15622, the system must demonstrate compliance with the functional‑safety lifecycle defined in ISO 26262‑1, including hazard identification, risk assessment, and verification of safety mechanisms.  The twin also adheres to the end‑to‑end latency constraints set out in 3GPP TS 22.185 for V2X services, ensuring that safety‑critical data is transmitted within the required delay budget (on the order of 10–15 ms) to support real‑time control decisions.  Safety‑critical measures encompass built‑in fail‑safe modes, redundancy of critical data streams, and formal safety validation procedures as mandated by ISO 26262.  These combined references guarantee that the system’s operation remains within the bounds of acceptable safety risk while meeting industry‑standard communication performance.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description accurately captures the physical twin as a '1/10‑scale autonomous vehicle' retrofitted with autonomy elements and an ACC system, as evidenced by the text: 'The physical twin (PT) is a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle... built off a Traxxas teleoperated vehicle... by retrofitting it with autonomy components' and 'physical architecture consists of three layers... lower level chassis... upper level chassis... autonomy components'. It correctly mentions the operational domain ('controlled environment for ACC development') and suitability factors ('small scale, dense sensor suite, and the ability to off‑load critical computational tasks to external resources'), supported by the text: 'DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient' and references to the sensor suite (LiDAR, Depth Camera). The extraction is highly specific and well-grounded in the sources.

The description correctly identifies the three primary services: Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, and Remote Management, providing detailed technical content for each. This is strongly supported by the source: 'To implement the ACC-DT, we develop the following three services through a DT. 1) Data Monitoring: Monitoring ACC-relevant data... contain a user interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports... display real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and detected object tracks'; '2) Computation Offloading: A DT can alleviate the computational burden... offloading only the ACC system from the vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead vehicle, then computes a desired speed...'; '3) Remote Management: ...enable the management and operation of ACC remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.' The extraction also correctly notes the latency constraints, referencing R7: 'maintain network latency of at most 100 ms'. The content is highly specific and detailed.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly lists several tools, frameworks, and enablers used for the implementation, such as ROS2, NVIDIA Jetpack, and programming languages. Supported by: 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware', 'NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the Software Development Kit (SDK)', 'Application modules are implemented in Python 3.8... C++... and MATLAB/Simulink'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

block_2_judge.reasoning:
The extraction accurately details the temporal constraints and requirements found in the text, specifically the 100 ms clock synchronization accuracy and the 100 ms network latency requirement, correctly attributing the reasons (hardware limitations and computation off-loading). It also correctly notes that no finer granularity is specified.

Supporting quotes:
- "Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms."
- "The DTS shall maintain network latency of at most 100 ms from the vehicle to the DT Entity."
- "To enable computation offloading, devices must share the same notion of time."
The document describes a single Digital Twin for a single vehicle and does not discuss multiple instances, hierarchical structures, or decentralized coordination mechanisms.

The extraction provides a highly detailed and accurate summary of the system's architecture based on ISO 23247. It correctly identifies the three primary entities (User, Digital Twin, Physical Twin) and their implementation as ROS2 packages. It accurately maps specific functionalities: User Interface split into '3D Visualization and Control Panel', data management in the Digital Twin Entity, and sensor/autonomous processing in the Physical Twin Entity. It also correctly notes the architectural deviation regarding the Physical Twin Entity and the lack of a natural mapping for Data Storage.

While the text mentions standards like ISO 15622 and 3GPP for defining system requirements, it does not describe integration with external systems, data exchange protocols with third parties, or integration patterns with other enterprise systems.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
